**Jan Mølby** (Danish pronunciation: [[ˈmølpy]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Danish "Help:IPA/Danish"); born 4 July 1963) is a Danish former professional [footballer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and manager. As a player, he was a [midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder_(association_football) "Midfielder (association football)") from 1982 to 1998. After starting his career with [Kolding](/wiki/Kolding_IF "Kolding IF"), he moved on to [Ajax](/wiki/AFC_Ajax "AFC Ajax") before spending twelve years playing in England with [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."). He was [capped](/wiki/Cap_(sport) "Cap (sport)") 33 times by [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team"), scoring twice.

After leaving Liverpool he became player-manager of [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C."), where he spent two years, and then managed [Kidderminster Harriers](/wiki/Kidderminster_Harriers_F.C. "Kidderminster Harriers F.C."), guiding them to promotion to the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") in 2000. He later had a brief spell as manager of [Hull City](/wiki/Hull_City_A.F.C. "Hull City A.F.C.") and then a brief spell back in charge of Kidderminster Harriers.

## Club career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jan_M%C3%B8lby&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Club career")]

Born in [Kolding](/wiki/Kolding "Kolding"),[[1]](#cite_note-Hugman-1) Mølby started his senior playing career at [Kolding](/wiki/Kolding_IF "Kolding IF"), before joining [Ajax](/wiki/AFC_Ajax "AFC Ajax") and then [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."). He made his Reds debut on 25 August 1984, in a 3–3 [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") draw with [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") at [Carrow Road](/wiki/Carrow_Road "Carrow Road"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) scoring his first goal for the club on 1 December of that year, in a 3–1 league defeat to [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") at [Stamford Bridge](/wiki/Stamford_Bridge_(stadium) "Stamford Bridge (stadium)").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Mølby began to establish himself as a regular and successful penalty taker, starting with two penalties converted at home to [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") in the league on 28 September 1985. Other fine performances included a brace in open play in a 3–0 home win over [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") in the league on 7 December, and two goals (one a penalty) as they eliminated [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") from the [Football League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup") in a 2–1 win at [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield "Anfield") in late November.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Ahead of the [1987–88 season](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_Liverpool_F.C._season "1987–88 Liverpool F.C. season"), Mølby broke his leg in pre-season training.[[6]](#cite_note-lfchistorypp-6) He was then arrested for [drink driving](/wiki/Drink_driving "Drink driving") in February 1988.[[6]](#cite_note-lfchistorypp-6)

In 1988–89, Mølby returned to regular first team football, playing in central defence in the absence of the injured [Alan Hansen](/wiki/Alan_Hansen "Alan Hansen"), and scoring the winning goal against Manchester United at Anfield in the second league game of the season. However, in October 1988 Mølby was sentenced to three months' imprisonment for reckless driving following an incident earlier in the year.[[7]](#cite_note-7) On his release, he struggled with further injuries and his general fitness.[[6]](#cite_note-lfchistorypp-6)

Over the first three Premier League seasons (Mølby's last), he started just over 30 games in total and all his goals came from penalties, including one in Liverpool's first game of the 1994–95 season when Mølby opened the scoring with a penalty in a 6–1 away win against Crystal Palace at [Selhurst Park](/wiki/Selhurst_Park "Selhurst Park").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Early in the 1995–96 season, manager [Roy Evans](/wiki/Roy_Evans "Roy Evans") loaned Mølby out to Barnsley and Norwich (where he scored once in the League Cup against Birmingham City).[[9]](#cite_note-9) In February 1996, Mølby left Liverpool to take over as player-manager of [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C."). Just before taking the job at the [Vetch Field](/wiki/Vetch_Field "Vetch Field"), [Ron Atkinson](/wiki/Ron_Atkinson "Ron Atkinson") unsuccessfully tried to sign him for [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

While at Liverpool, he scored a total of 62 goals, 42 of which were from penalties. During Mølby's time with Liverpool, he only failed to score three times from the penalty spot (penalties against Sheffield Wednesday and QPR in 1985–86 and Chelsea in 1989–90 were saved). His record as a penalty-taker in the top flight is thought to be second only to [Matthew Le Tissier](/wiki/Matthew_Le_Tissier "Matthew Le Tissier"). Mølby held the club record of most penalties scored by a Liverpool player until [Steven Gerrard](/wiki/Steven_Gerrard "Steven Gerrard") surpassed his record in August 2014.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Managerial career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jan_M%C3%B8lby&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Managerial career")]

Mølby became manager of [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C.") in February 1996 but was dismissed in October 1997 along with his assistant, [Billy Ayre](/wiki/Billy_Ayre "Billy Ayre"). Mølby had taken Swansea to the [Division Three](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") playoff final five months earlier, but they lost to a last-minute goal by [Northampton Town](/wiki/Northampton_Town_F.C. "Northampton Town F.C.")'s [John Frain](/wiki/John_Frain "John Frain"). A dismal start to the [1997–98 season](/wiki/1997-98_in_English_football "1997-98 in English football") had seen Swansea struggling near the foot of the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League"), and the board decided that it was time for a new manager to be appointed, asserting that the team's good performances the previous season were more down to Mølby's qualities as a top class player, rather than as a manager.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Following [Kenny Jackett](/wiki/Kenny_Jackett "Kenny Jackett")'s resignation as Swansea manager in early 2007, Mølby was linked with a return to the club. However, Mølby responded by saying that he is unlikely to ever return to football management.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Recent years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jan_M%C3%B8lby&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Recent years")]

In April 2009, Mølby was made an 'Honorary [Scouser](/wiki/Scouser "Scouser")' by the [Lord Mayor of Liverpool](/wiki/Lord_Mayor_of_Liverpool "Lord Mayor of Liverpool").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Mølby appears on a podcast called "Mølby on the Spot" presented by Trevor Downey.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

A [running gag](/wiki/Running_gag "Running gag") of comedian [Troy Hawke](/wiki/Troy_Hawke "Troy Hawke") ([Milo McCabe](/wiki/Milo_McCabe "Milo McCabe")) as he performs as a street [greeter](/wiki/Greeter "Greeter") is that he was sent by Mølby, the "non-consensual CEO of the Greeter's Guild." The two men met at the public unveiling of a Mølby mural in Liverpool in March 2023.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Mølby made a cameo voice-only appearance as a [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball") commentator in the fifth episode of [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two") comedy *[Small Prophets](/wiki/Small_Prophets "Small Prophets")* in 2026.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
